author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
The Third Calumny,—The Assertion that Conjugal Intercourse is Condemned.
title: The Third Calumny,—The Assertion that Conjugal Intercourse is Condemned.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “They say also,” says he, “that sexual impulse and the intercourse of married people were devised by the devil, and that therefore those who are born innocent are guilty, and that it is the work of the devil, not of God, that they are born of this diabolical intercourse.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And this, without any ambiguity, is Manicheism.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Nay, as I say that marriage was appointed by God for the sake of the ordinance of the begetting of children, so I say that the propagation of children to be begotten could not have taken place without sexual impulse, and without intercourse of husband and wife, even in Paradise, if children were begotten there.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But whether such impulse and intercourse would have existed, as is now the case with shameful lust, if no one had sinned, here is the question concerning which I shall argue hereafter, if God will.
